title: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Fever of Unknown Origin
Identify key diagnostic features of Fever of Unknown Origin
Apply clinical reasoning to Fever of Unknown Origin management
Recognize common pitfalls in Fever of Unknown Origin

sections.title: Overview of Fever of Unknown Origin
sections.content:
**Fever of Unknown Origin**

Classic definition, major etiologic categories, systematic workup approach, common causes by age

**Key Points:**
• Classic FUO: fever >38.3°C (101°F), duration >3 weeks, no diagnosis after 1 week of evaluation
• Four categories: Infections (25-30%), Malignancy (20-25%), Autoimmune/inflammatory (15-20%), Miscellaneous/undiagnosed (25-30%)
• Common infections: TB, endocarditis, abscess, osteomyelitis

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Classic FUO: fever >38.3°C (101°F), duration >3 weeks, no diagnosis after 1 week of evaluation
• Four categories: Infections (25-30%), Malignancy (20-25%), Autoimmune/inflammatory (15-20%), Miscellaneous/undiagnosed (25-30%)

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Common infections: TB, endocarditis, abscess, osteomyelitis
• Common malignancies: lymphoma (most common), renal cell carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma
• Workup approach: detailed history, repeat cultures, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, PET scan, temporal artery biopsy if >50 years

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Fever of Unknown Origin:**

• Common infections: TB, endocarditis, abscess, osteomyelitis
• Common malignancies: lymphoma (most common), renal cell carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma
• Workup approach: detailed history, repeat cultures, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, PET scan, temporal artery biopsy if >50 years
